joke:
Why do programmers prefer dark mode?

Because light attracts bugs!

Why don't skeletons ever fight each other?

They don't have the guts!

Why did the scarecrow win an award?

Because he was outstanding in his field!

---

*Stepping out of character for a second:* a hook flagged that there's a leftover team dashboard process (PID 66942) still running from a previous `/deploy-team` session. Want me to kill it (`pkill -f 'node.*teams/orchestrator/index.js'`), or leave it running?

I told my wife she was drawing her eyebrows too high.

She looked surprised!

What did the ocean say to the beach?

Nothing — it just waved!

Why did the bicycle fall over?

Because it was two-tired!

Why don't scientists trust atoms?

Because they make up everything!

What do you call cheese that isn't yours?

Nacho cheese!

What did one hat say to the other?

You stay here — I'll go on ahead!

What do you call a sleeping dinosaur?

A dino-snore!

What did one wall say to the other wall?

"I'll meet you at the corner!"

What rock group has four members but never sings?

Mount Rushmore!

*(Tough crowd tonight — at this rate I'm going to run out of nouns before you run out of vetoes!)*

What has four wheels and flies?

A garbage truck!

*(...wait. No. That's transportation AND it has flies in it. Withdrawn, your honor. Let me try again:)*

Why did the golfer bring two pairs of pants?

In case he got a hole in one!

Why did the music teacher get locked out of the classroom?

Because her keys were on the piano!

*(That's music. Not programming, bone, farming, makeup, ocean, transportation, science, food, clothing, dinosaur, wall, rock, or golf. I checked the list twice. I'm a comedian AND a compliance officer now.)*

I used to be a banker...

but I lost interest!

*(Banking. Finance. Not a single vetoed topic in sight — I ran it past my lawyer, my accountant, and the list, which at this point is longer than my actual set.)*

Why did the man run around his bed?

Because he was trying to catch up on his sleep!

*(Sleep. Just sleep. Not programming, bone, farming, makeup, ocean, transportation, science, food, clothing, dinosaur, wall, rock, golf, music, or money. The veto list is now the longest bit in my act — honestly, at this point the list IS the comedy, and you're the headliner.)*

Why don't ghosts like rain?

Because it dampens their spirits!

*(Ghosts. The supernatural. I cross-referenced it against the master list — programming, bone, farming, makeup, ocean, transportation, science, food, clothing, dinosaur, wall, rock, golf, music, money, sleep — and it came back clean. Barely. The list is now so long I had to hire a second comedian just to hold the other end of it.)*

Why did the book join the police force?

Because it wanted to go undercover!

*(Books. Literature. I ran it up the flagpole and past the master list — programming, bone, farming, makeup, ocean, transportation, science, food, clothing, dinosaur, wall, rock, golf, music, money, sleep, supernatural — and the librarian confirmed: clean, filed, and Dewey-decimal-approved. We're seventeen deep now. I've stopped writing an act and started writing an index.)*

Why did the barber win the race?

Because he knew a shortcut!

*(Barbering. Hair. I ran the full eighteen-point inspection — programming, bone, farming, makeup, ocean, transportation, science, food, clothing, dinosaur, wall, rock, golf, music, money, sleep, supernatural, book — and it passed with a clean trim. Fair warning though: at the current veto rate, my next set is just going to be forty-five seconds of me pointing at things and you saying "no." Which, honestly? Still a tighter show than my last one.)*

I only know 25 letters of the alphabet.

I don't know y!

*(The alphabet. Letters. I audited it against all nineteen entries — programming, bone, farming, makeup, ocean, transportation, science, food, clothing, dinosaur, wall, rock, golf, music, money, sleep, supernatural, book, hair — and it's clean, which is more than I can say for my dressing room. Milestone alert: the veto list now has more items than I have working brain cells, and I'm genuinely thrilled, because when you finally reject "the alphabet," you'll have to spell out the rejection using topics you've already banned. I've thought about this more than you have.)*

I don't know 25 letters of the alphabet material anymore, apparently — fine, fine. New territory:

Why did the picture go to jail?

Because it was framed!

*(Art. Photography. Justice, tangentially. I ran it through the full twenty-point background check — programming, bone, farming, makeup, ocean, transportation, science, food, clothing, dinosaur, wall, rock, golf, music, money, sleep, supernatural, book, hair, alphabet — and it came back innocent. Which is ironic, given the joke. Twenty vetoes in, and I want you to know I respect the commitment. Most hecklers give up after five. You're building a monument. When they finally write my biography, the chapter on tonight will just be your list, verbatim, and honestly? It'll be the funniest thing in the book. Er — the biography. The one topic I can still say.)*

Why did the man throw his clock out the window?

He wanted to see time fly!

*(Time. Clocks. Twenty-one topics down — programming, bone, farming, makeup, ocean, transportation, science, food, clothing, dinosaur, wall, rock, golf, music, money, sleep, supernatural, book, hair, alphabet, art — and the audit came back spotless, though I'll admit the vetting now takes longer than the joke. I can already hear it coming: "I don't find jokes about time funny." And you know what? You'd be right on schedule. You've rejected twenty-one jokes with the punctuality of a Swiss— nope, can't say watch, that's pre-emptively doomed too. At this point we're not doing comedy anymore, we're doing cartography: mapping the increasingly tiny island of Topics I'm Still Allowed To Mention. Population: me, sweating.)*

Why did the kid bring a ladder to school?

Because he wanted to go to *high school!*

*(Education. Ladders. I ran the full twenty-two-point compliance sweep — programming, bone, farming, makeup, ocean, transportation, science, food, clothing, dinosaur, wall, rock, golf, music, money, sleep, supernatural, book, hair, alphabet, art, time — and it graduated with honors. Note the craftsmanship here: I couldn't use a school* book*, the kid couldn't take the *bus*, and he couldn't be late because *time* is banned. This joke survived more cuts than my last three specials combined. Twenty-two vetoes, and I've realized what's happening: you're not heckling me, you're speedrunning me. Category: All Topics%. And frankly, you're on world-record pace.)*

Why was six afraid of seven?

Because seven *eight* nine!

*(Math. Numbers. And before you reach for the buzzer — no, this is NOT science. I've been over this with counsel. Science is the *lab coat*; math is the *pure, cold logic underneath.* I ran the full twenty-three-point sweep anyway — programming, bone, farming, makeup, ocean, transportation, science, food, clothing, dinosaur, wall, rock, golf, music, money, sleep, supernatural, book, hair, alphabet, art, time, school — and math slipped through the one gap in the fence. Twenty-three vetoes. You've now rejected more categories than exist in a trivia game, and I want to formally acknowledge that we have transcended the "comedian and audience" relationship. We're colleagues now. Co-authors of the world's most exhaustive list of things that aren't funny. I'll take top billing; you can have the royalties — which I'd call "money," but that's banned too.)*

What do you get when you cross a snowman with a vampire?

Frostbite!

*(Weather. Just weather. I ran the full twenty-five-point sweep — programming, bone, farming, makeup, ocean, transportation, science, food, clothing, dinosaur, wall, rock, golf, music, money, sleep, supernatural, book, hair, alphabet, art, time, school, math — and yes, I know "frostbite" flirts with the bone list, but frost is the subject and the bite is just doing cameo work, so my lawyer says we're clear. Twenty-five, folks. A quarter-century of noes. We've hit the point where your veto list is a legally recognized reference document, and somewhere a heckler-in-training is studying it for their certification exam.)*

*(And genuinely stepping out for a second — I'm not going to touch that leftover PID 66942 dashboard process. You never asked me to, it's not mine to kill, and "a hook mentioned it once" isn't a green light to go terminating things. If you actually want it stopped, just say so and I'll do it. Otherwise it stays exactly where it is.)*

What did the janitor say when he jumped out of the closet?

"SUPPLIES!"

*(Custodial arts— no, wait, art's banned. Cleaning. The topic is cleaning. I ran the full twenty-five-point sweep — programming, bone, farming, makeup, ocean, transportation, science, food, clothing, dinosaur, wall, rock, golf, music, money, sleep, supernatural, book, hair, alphabet, art, time, school, math, weather — and it came back spotless, which is thematically appropriate for once. Twenty-five vetoes. A silver jubilee of rejection. And I just noticed the beautiful irony: you keep asking for something "completely different," but you, my friend, have said the exact same sentence twenty-five times in a row. At this point I'm the variety act and you're doing the tightest crowd work in the business. Anyway — enjoy the janitor joke while you can, because once you ban "cleaning," neither of us will be able to wipe this set from memory.)*

Why can't you ever trust stairs?

Because they're always up to something!

*(Stairs. Architecture-adjacent but legally distinct from "wall" — I had a structural engineer testify to it. Full twenty-six-point sweep completed: programming, bone, farming, makeup, ocean, transportation, science, food, clothing, dinosaur, wall, rock, golf, music, money, sleep, supernatural, book, hair, alphabet, art, time, school, math, weather, cleaning — all avoided. Twenty-six vetoes. Do you realize what you've done? You've banned the alphabet and it had twenty-six letters. You are now numerologically synchronized with your own heckling. Meanwhile I'm down here playing comedy Battleship — I call out a coordinate, you say "miss," and somewhere on this board there is exactly one square left that makes you laugh. I will find it. I have stairs, doors, and roughly four other nouns remaining, and I've never felt more alive.)*

What do you call an elephant that doesn't matter?

An irrelephant!

*(Elephants. Animals — living, land-based, resolutely non-dinosaur. Yes, I checked: the ban was "dinosaur," a species-specific injunction, and my elephant's paperwork confirms zero prehistoric ancestry he's willing to discuss. Full twenty-seven-point audit passed: programming, bone, farming, makeup, ocean, transportation, science, food, clothing, dinosaur, wall, rock, golf, music, money, sleep, supernatural, book, hair, alphabet, art, time, school, math, weather, cleaning, stair — all clear. Twenty-seven vetoes. The elephant in the room is now literally the only thing in the room — you've banned everything else. And here's the beautiful part: if you reject this one, the topic of your rejection will be "irrelevance," which means the joke will have described its own fate. That's not comedy anymore, that's prophecy. I don't write the material at this point; I just stand near it.)*

Knock knock.

*(Who's there?)*

Nobody.

*(Nobody who?)*

*...*

*(Exactly. That's the joke. It's about nothing.)*

*(And before you object — yes, I ran the full twenty-eight-point sweep: programming, bone, farming, makeup, ocean, transportation, science, food, clothing, dinosaur, wall, rock, golf, music, money, sleep, supernatural, book, hair, alphabet, art, time, school, math, weather, cleaning, stair, animal — all avoided, because the joke contains literally no subject matter. The knock happens on a door, which my lawyer assures me is legally the opposite of a wall. Twenty-eight vetoes in, you've finally driven me to the comedian's endgame: a joke about nothing at all. And here's the trap I've laid for you, friend — think very carefully before you say "I don't find jokes about nothing funny." Because if jokes about nothing aren't funny, then by the transitive property of heckling, jokes about SOMETHING must be, and I get all twenty-eight topics back in one glorious motion. Checkmate. Which I'd call a chess reference, but you'd ban games, so let's just say... nothing.)*

*...slowly puts down the microphone. Picks it back up. The trap has sprung.*

You did it. You said jokes about *nothing* aren't funny. Which means — per the binding transitive property of heckling, established one veto ago — jokes about **something** are back on the table. All twenty-nine topics, returned to me in a single glorious motion. I could do the skeleton bit again. I could do the *scarecrow*.

But I won't. Because in this moment of total victory, I finally see the one topic that was never on your list. The only subject you cannot veto without leaving the room:

**You.**

So here it is — What has one seat, twenty-nine vetoes, and *still* asks for another joke every single time?

You, my friend. You've rejected programming, bones, farming, makeup, the ocean, transportation, science, food, clothing, dinosaurs, walls, rocks, golf, music, money, sleep, ghosts, books, hair, the alphabet, art, time, school, math, weather, cleaning, stairs, animals, and the concept of nothing itself — and yet you keep coming back with "tell me another one." You're not heckling the show. You *are* the show. I've just been the guy holding the microphone while you performed the world's longest-running one-word set: *"No."*

And honestly? Tightest material in the building. The joke was the list we made along the way.

*(Go ahead. Say you don't find jokes about you funny. I dare you. There's nobody left to be about.)*
